प्रयत्नाद्यतमानस्तु योगी संशुद्धकिल्बिषः ।
अनेकजन्मसंसिद्धस्ततो याति परां गतिम् ॥ ४५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
prayatnād yatamānas tu yogī saṁśuddha-kilbiṣaḥ |
aneka-janma-saṁsiddhas tato yāti parāṁ gatim || 45 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: And when the yogī engages himself with sincere endeavor in making further progress, being washed of all contaminations, then ultimately, achieving perfection after many, many births of practice, he attains the supreme goal.

And when the yogi engages himself with sincere endeavor in making further progress, being washed of all contaminations, then ultimately, achieving perfection after many, many births of practice, he attains the supreme goal.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 821 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A person born in a particularly righteous, aristocratic or sacred family becomes conscious of his favorable condition for executing yoga practice. With determination, therefore, he begins his unfinished task, and thus he completely cleanses himself of all material contaminations.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.45 The yogi, the man of Knowledge; yatamanah, applying himself; prayatnat, assiduously, i.e. striving more intensely; and as a result, samsuddha-kilbisah, becoming purified from sin; and aneka-janma-samsiddhah, attaining perfection through many births- gathering together tendencies littl
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-45-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as describing the yogi (the knower) who applies himself with assiduous striving (prayatnāt yatamānaḥ), thereby becoming purified of sin (saṁśuddha-kilbiṣaḥ), and — through the gathering-together of tendencies across many births (aneka-janma-saṁsiddhaḥ) — reaches the supreme goal (parām gatim). The compounding of saṁskāras across lives is explicit in Shankara's reading.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.45 Because of such excellence of Yoga, through accumulation of merit collected in many births the Yogin striving earnestly, becomes cleansed from stains. Having become perfected, he reaches the supreme state, even though he had once gone astray. Sri Krsna now speaks of the superiority of
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-45-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja highlights the *excellence of Yoga* itself: through merit accumulated across many births, the earnestly-striving yogi becomes cleansed of stains, perfected, and reaches the supreme state — *even though he had once gone astray*. The fall is explicitly accommodated; the destination is reached anyway.

positions.rendering: achieving perfection after many, many births of practice
attaining perfection through many births — gathering together tendencies
through accumulation of merit collected in many births

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three render the compound aneka-janma-saṁsiddhaḥ at multi-life scope. Shankara emphasises the *gathering of tendencies* (saṁskāra-accumulation); Ramanuja emphasises the *accumulation of merit* across births; Prabhupada emphasises *many, many births of practice*. The structural claim is uncontroversial: perfection is reached through multi-life compounding, not single-life force-of-effort. This is the verse's STRETCHED dimension for engineering analog.

literal_meaning: But the yogi, striving with effort, purified of contamination, perfected through many births, then reaches the supreme goal.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.45 closes the consolation-arc that began at 6.37 and reached its diagnostic at 6.40-44. The yogi who fell from the practice does not lose the work; the prior practice carries forward; through accumulated effort across many births (aneka-janma-saṁsiddhaḥ) the practitioner is purified and reaches the supreme destination (parām gatim). Shankara: gathering of tendencies across births. Ramanuja: accumulation of merit across many births, and 'even though he had once gone astray' the destination is reached. Prabhupada: 'achieving perfection after many, many births of practice.' The verse's metaphysical scope is multi-life and explicit; engineering analog must operate at single-life-substrate scope (decade of compounding practice produces the senior's effortlessness) and tag STRETCHED.
